prompt: I'm modeling the effect of a workplace safety program on injury rates across 30 factories. I have monthly injury counts for 2 years before and after the program was introduced, plus number of employees per factory. I have strong prior beliefs from a previous study that the program reduces injuries by about 25% (rate ratio around 0.75), so I want to encode that as an informative prior. But a colleague challenged me, saying my prior might be driving the results. Can you build the model and check whether my conclusions are sensitive to the prior choice?
assertions: Uses a count likelihood (Poisson or NegativeBinomial) with log-link
Encodes an informative prior on the treatment effect reflecting the ~25% reduction belief
Imports psense_summary from arviz_stats and runs it on the InferenceData
Interprets the psense_summary output — mentions CJS values or sensitivity diagnosis for the treatment effect parameter
Discusses whether the informative prior flags as sensitive and what that means for the conclusions
Does NOT reflexively weaken the prior just because sensitivity is flagged — justifies the choice or explains the tradeoff
Imports plot_psense_dist from arviz_plots (not arviz_stats) for visual sensitivity diagnostics
Uses coords and dims in the PyMC model definition
Reports 94% HDI (not 95% CI) for parameter estimates
Generates a companion analysis notes file interpreting the sensitivity results
